en.extract:
The black-capped tyrannulet (Tyranniscus  nigrocapillus) is a small passerine bird in the tyrant flycatcher family Tyrannidae. It is found in Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Venezuela. Its natural habitat is subtropical or tropical moist montane forests.


Taxonomy
The black-capped tyrannulet was formally described in 1845 by the French ornithologist Frédéric de Lafresnaye under the binomial name Tyrannulus nigrocapillus. He specified the type locality as "Bogotam", now Bogotá in Columbia. The specific epithet combines the Latin niger meaning "black" with -capillus meaning "-capped". The species was formerly placed in the genus Phyllomyias  but when a molecular phylogenetic study published in 2020 found that this genus was paraphyletic, the black-capped tyrannulet and two other tyrannulets were moved to the resurrected genus Tyranniscus that has been introduced in 1860 by the German ornithologists Jean Cabanis and Ferdinand Heine.
Three subspecies are recognised:

T. n. flavimentum (Chapman, 1912) – Santa Marta Mountains (northeast Colombia)
T. n. nigrocapillus (Lafresnaye, 1845) – west Colombia and Ecuador to central Peru
T. n. aureus (Zimmer, JT, 1941) – west Venezuela


References

The '''black-capped tyrannulet''' ('''''Tyranniscus  nigrocapillus''''') is a small [[passerine]] bird in the tyrant flycatcher family [[Tyrannidae]]. It is found in [[Colombia]], [[Ecuador]], [[Peru]], and [[Venezuela]]. Its natural [[habitat]] is subtropical or tropical moist [[montane forest]]s.

==Taxonomy==
The black-capped tyrannulet was [[species description|formally described]] in 1845 by the French ornithologist [[Frédéric de Lafresnaye]] under the [[binomial name]] ''Tyrannulus nigrocapillus''. He specified the [[type locality (biology)|type locality]] as "Bogotam", now [[Bogotá]] in Columbia.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=de Lafresnaye | first=Frédéric | author-link=Frédéric de Lafresnaye | year=1845 | title=Description de quelques oiseaux nouveaux | language=French | journal=Revue Zoologique | volume=8 | pages=337–342 [341] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/2327565 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Traylor | editor-first=Melvin A. Jr | author-link=Melvin Alvah Traylor Jr. | year=1979 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=8 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=8 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14500785 }}</ref> The specific epithet combines the [[Latin]] ''niger'' meaning "black" with ''-capillus'' meaning "-capped".<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=nigrocapillus | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=nigrocapillus | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=17 November 2024 }}</ref> The species was formerly placed in the genus ''[[Phyllomyias ]]'' but when a [[molecular phylogenetic]] study published in 2020 found that this genus was [[paraphyletic]], the black-capped tyrannulet and two other tyrannulets were moved to the resurrected genus ''[[Tyranniscus]]'' that has been introduced in 1860 by the German ornithologists [[Jean Cabanis]] and [[Ferdinand Heine]].<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=August 2024 | title=Tyrant flycatchers | work=IOC World Bird List Version 14.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/flycatchers/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=17 November 2024 }}</ref><ref name=harvey>{{Cite journal | last1=Harvey | first1=M.G. | display-authors=etal | date=2020 | title=The evolution of a tropical biodiversity hotspot | journal=Science | volume=370 | issue=6522 | pages=1343–1348 | doi=10.1126/science.aaz6970 | hdl=10138/329703 | hdl-access=free }} A high resolution version of the phylogenetic tree in Figure 1 is available from the first author's website [http://www.harveybirdlab.org/docs/Harveyetal2020_Fig1_tree_HiRes.pdf here].</ref>

Three [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name=ioc/>
* ''T. n. flavimentum'' ([[Frank Chapman (ornithologist)|Chapman]], 1912) – [[Sierra Nevada de Santa Marta|Santa Marta Mountains]] (northeast Colombia)
* ''T. n. nigrocapillus'' ([[Frédéric de Lafresnaye|Lafresnaye]], 1845) – west Colombia and Ecuador to central Peru
* ''T. n. aureus'' ([[John T. Zimmer|Zimmer, JT]], 1941) – west Venezuela
